text: Welcome to This is My Architecture.
My name is Andrea and I'm here with Mark from Extreme Reach.
Hi Mark, welcome to the show.
Hi Andrea, thank you.
Tell us about Extreme Reach, what do you guys do?
Well, Extreme Reach is the bridge between the content originators and the media outlets.
Wonderful.
And content is advertisements.
Advertisement.
So today we're going to talk about the Adbridge platform.
Yes.
And can you talk us high level, what is Adbridge platform?
Well, Adbridge is a bridge between these content originators and these media outlets.
You have to have a system in an ecosystem or a system that supports the ecosystem that's
in the middle that gets this content from point A to point B.
I see.
But there's a lot of in-between steps along the way.
I see.
So the bridge between the content originators and outlets.
Okay, so walk us through kind of a life cycle of, you know, a content originator generates
or has a task to complete.
What happens first?
Sure, sure.
What's the first?
You're going to have an advertiser or a brand that's here and they are the ones that are
going to be bringing the content, publishing house and other things.
But you'll have, they'll be bringing content into our system.
And the way they'll do that is a variety of methods including direct upload through the
UI.
So, and through an API interface or through a Java agent.
So I may be able to just throw those here.
You know, this sort of Java API UI upload, right?
And they're going to be connecting, you know, first they're going to be hitting the ELB,
right?
Which is monitored by a WAF, a web application firewall, making sure that we're blocking any
promiscuous traffic.
And then we're going to go to the app tier, right?
We're going to send them to the app tier over here.
And then through the app tier is where, that's the primary user interface.
It's where our API lives and the interface for the Java upload or download, things like
that.
So they're all checking into the app tier.
But then eventually they're going to be uploading into S3 and generally that's going
to be a direct upload.
Oh, wonderful.
It's either going to be from this Java application, which we call a media uploader, or direct input
via the UI to S3 storage.
I see.
This is our ad cloud, so we store everything in S3.
Wonderful.
So you said the app server, I assume this is the web server fronting with the UI for the
users to use.
And so in this context, we're talking about massive scale, I can assume.
Absolutely.
So can you give us an idea of, you know, in terms of uploads to S3 and interaction with
UI, what are we talking about here in terms of scale?
Well, definitely we handle a large volume of advertisements in the United States.
We handle approximately 80% of that ad traffic that's going from point A to point B. So it's
a huge volume just of the initial ad. But then we have to make all these different versions
of the ad that can be broadcast ready in all sorts of different media outlets. And we do
that using transcoding servers.
Okay.
So we have a fleet of our own.
So this is your app servers, actual, after the web tier.
Yeah. So after the web tier, these are back-ends. So now I'm going to describe some back-end
things. And then we also have what we call our batch servers. And these run all sorts of
various work applications to move work around the system. There's a variety of custom made applications
that we have here. Transcoding is using conventional transcoding software, popular transcoding software,
but it's very customized to make sure that this asset that comes into the system is now
converted into a pristine format through the transcoders. We also perform a QC work on it
as well. So I'll just throw that here as well. Quality control, making sure that the video.
Quality control. Okay. So you have that back-end application tier essentially doing all the processing. Now I also see cloud front, right? So you have a point of
entry with cloud front. We talked about S3. What is this used for?
Right. So deliveries actually are primarily through S3. So those would go to the media outlets over here.
Okay. And some of those deliveries are going to be delivered via cloud front using an S3 front-end for that, right? Or I'm sorry, an S3 source for that.
Yeah. But we also have digital video advertising as well, which we're not talking about today, but we do all of those deliveries through cloud front.
I see. All of those digital videos.
Okay. So digital media distribution through cloud front.
Totally through here. Everything else is.
And then conventional TV, ad commercials, primarily through S3, some of which is through content.
Wonderful. Cloud front. Don't worry.
Yeah. So you might have different content originators then providing different content into S3. How do you prioritize?
How do you deal with the massive scale as it progresses through the web tier to the application tier?
Great question. We were an early adopter of S3 and SQS back in 2009 when it was not cool to be on the cloud, but then it became cool.
But we've utilized SQS for asynchronous, right?
Okay. Workloads.

So what we do is we populate queues and then these particular applications running on the batch server, the transcode, the QC, even some that are running on the app, are checking these queues to say, do you have anything for me? Do you have anything for me?

And then they all process that. And what we've done with these queues is when we have hundreds of these queues, we've prioritized some of those queues as if it's a time sensitive delivery where that advertiser or content originator needs to get that out to a media outlet within an hour, then we're going to use a high priority queue to process that.
I see. Well, let's talk about the media outlets.
Sure.
Now, content has been put on S3. You have it processed through the transcoding, the batch processing, QC. What happens then? How do you get it to the media outlets?

Great. Well, I'll share that we wouldn't transcode the content until we're ready for delivery. So within a certain timeframe, we're going to grab that master file. We're going to convert it into all the different versions.

Those versions are now going to get stored into S3. And then those are going to be queued up for delivery for the media outlets. And those media outlets are all either using the UI interface or they're utilizing our API or our Java application.
We have a Java downloader that we also have that could be checking their accounts. And that would be pulling down. And those are all checking in here as well.
Wonderful. Pulling that media down. And it'll pull it down directly from S3. So once that content is queued, it will come directly from S3 in the most accessible region, East or West region.

Wonderful. This is fantastic. Thank you, Mark, for walking us to kind of this architecture where you have content originators using the UI and APIs to interact with your web application tier, uploading content on S3.
You also have digital media content through CloudFront, also making sure that it's highly secure and intrusion is detected in time with the firewall WAF in this case.

And then also the whole back end with SQS for prioritizing the task in the queue, as well as transcoding and doing batch processing to further process and then make it accessible to the media outlets.
Thank you so much for sharing this. And I also thank viewers for watching. This is my architecture.
